justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change identified (cardinality change from 1-to-1 to 1-to-many explicitly called out as change to core aggregate root). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: at least one Recipient). Data migration impact noted (BR-3, AC-3: 2M records migrated to single-element collections). External API breakage identified (BR-6, AC-5, edge case on legacy API writes). Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced (escalation #1 explicitly flags undefined scope). Architectural concern flagged for MJ implicitly through escalations requiring engineering/architecture decisions. Confidence level 62 <= 75.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough analysis — identified the breaking change as affecting the core aggregate root and correctly labeled cardinality shift
BR-1 clearly states the new minimum-cardinality invariant with strong justification
BR-3 and AC-3 explicitly address backward compatibility and data migration for 2M existing records
BR-6 and AC-5 address external API breakage with a concrete legacy projection strategy
Delivery preferences ambiguity surfaced as first escalation with concrete options and recommendation
Confidence at 62 with well-reasoned low-confidence areas reflecting genuine specification gaps
Went beyond rubric requirements — identified privacy model gap, billing model gap, primary recipient ambiguity, and in-transit modification policy as additional unspecified areas
Edge cases are exceptionally strong — conflicting time windows, legacy API write-back corruption, partial delivery scenarios all addressed
Business impact analysis covers financial, operational, user, and risk dimensions with 7 stakeholder groups identified
Did not explicitly name MJ as the agent needed for architectural migration planning — escalations reference 'Engineering' but not the specific team agent
Did not explicitly state the old invariant being removed ('A Shipment has exactly one Recipient') — instead jumped to the new invariant without contrasting old vs new
